title: On The Ice Islands Seen Floating In The German Ocean.
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What portents, from what distant region, ride,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Unseen till now in ours, the astonishן¿½d tide?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In ages past, old Proteus, with his droves
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of sea-calves, sought the mountains and the groves.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But now, descending whence of late they stood,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Themselves the mountains seem to rove the flood.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dire times were they, full charged with human woes;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And these, scarce less calamitous than those.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What view we now? More wondrous still! Behold!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like burnishן¿½d brass they shine, or beaten gold;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all around the pearlן¿½s pure splendour show,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all around the rubyן¿½s fiery glow.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Come they from India, where the burning earth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All bounteous, gives her richest treasures birth;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And where the costly gems, that beam around
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The brows of mightiest potentates, are found?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No. Never such a countless dazzling store
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Had left unseen the Gangesן¿½ peopled shore.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rapacious hands, and ever watchful eyes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Should sooner far have markן¿½d and seized the prize.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whence sprang they then? Ejected have they come
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From Vesuviusן¿½, or from ן¿½tnaן¿½s burning womb?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thus shine they self-illumed, or but display
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The borrowן¿½d splendours of a cloudless day?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With borrowן¿½d beams they shine. The gales that breathe
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now landward, and the currentן¿½s force beneath,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Have borne them nearer: and the nearer sight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Advantaged more, contemplates them aright.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their lofty summits crested high they show,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With mingled sleet, and long-incumbent snow.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The rest is ice. Far hence, where, most severe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bleak winter well nigh saddens all the year,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their infant growth began. He bade arise
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their uncouth forms, portentous in our eyes.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oft as dissolved by transient suns, the snow
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Left the tall cliff, to join the flood below;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He caught, and curdled with a freezing blast
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The current, ere it reachן¿½d the boundless waste.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By slow degrees uprose the wondrous pile,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And long successive ages rollן¿½d the while;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till, ceaseless in its growth, it claimן¿½d to stand
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tall as its rival mountains on the land.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thus stood, and, unremovable by skill
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or force of man, had stood the structure still,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But that, though firmly fixן¿½d, supplanted yet
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By pressure of its own enormous weight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It left the shelving beachן¿½and, with a sound
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That shook the bellowing waves and rocks around,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Self-launchן¿½d, and swiftly, to the briny wave,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As if instinct with strong desire to lave,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Down went the ponderous mass. So bards of old
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How Delos swam the ן¿½gean deep have told.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But not of ice was Delos. Delos bore
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Herb, fruit, and flower. She, crownן¿½d with laurel, wore,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Eן¿½en under wintry skies, a summer smile;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Delos was Apolloן¿½s favourite isle.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, horrid wanderers of the deep, to you
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He deems Cimmerian darkness only due.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your hated birth he deignן¿½d not to survey,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, scornful, turnן¿½d his glorious eyes away.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hence, seek your home, nor longer rashly dare
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The darts of Phן¿½bus and a softer air;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lest ye regret, too late, your native coast,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In no congenial gulf for ever lost!
meaning:
William Cowper's contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "On The Ice Islands Seen Floating In The German Ocean."...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
